introduction: Number Series questions ask you to spot the hidden rule that links a sequence of numbers and then find the next term, a missing term, or the wrong term. These patterns appear constantly in placement and bank exams because they test pattern recognition under time pressure.
explanation:
CONCEPT

A number series is an ordered list of numbers generated by one or more arithmetic, geometric, or mixed rules. Common patterns include constant difference (arithmetic), constant ratio (geometric), squares/cubes, prime numbers, alternating two series, and digit-based tricks.

HOW TO SOLVE

1) Scan consecutive differences: a2−a1, a3−a2, …
2) If differences are constant, it is arithmetic. If differences themselves form a pattern (e.g. +2, +4, +6), use second-order differences.
3) Check ratios for geometric series.
4) Test squares, cubes, primes, or factorial-linked growth.
5) Split into odd and even positions when the series seems irregular.
6) Confirm the rule on every term before choosing the answer.

IMPORTANT RULES

• One consistent rule must explain the whole series.
• Alternating series = two interleaved patterns.
• Wrong-number questions: find the term that breaks the rule.
• Keep units and signs careful with negative differences.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Stopping after checking only the first two gaps.
2) Forcing a complicated rule when a simple one works.
3) Mixing up squares (n²) with near-squares (n²±1).
4) Ignoring an interleaved second series.
5) Arithmetic slips when differences grow quickly.

formulas.title: Second-order difference
formulas.content: If first differences increase by a constant k, next term = last + (last_diff + k)

formulas.title: Square / cube patterns
formulas.content: n², n²±1, n³, n³±1, or (n² + n)

formulas.title: Alternating series
formulas.content: Odd positions follow rule A; even positions follow rule B

shortcutTips.title: Difference first
shortcutTips.tip: Always write consecutive differences before guessing fancy patterns.

shortcutTips.title: Check squares quickly
shortcutTips.tip: Memorize 1–20 squares and 1–10 cubes to spot them instantly.

shortcutTips.title: Split odd/even
shortcutTips.tip: If gaps look chaotic, list odd-position and even-position terms separately.

shortcutTips.title: Verify all terms
shortcutTips.tip: A candidate rule is wrong if it fails on even one term.

shortcutTips.title: Wrong term hunt
shortcutTips.tip: For odd-one-out series, test which single change restores a clean rule.

steps: Differences double each time: 4, 8, 16, 32 → next difference 64.
Replacing 150 with 160 restores the geometric pattern with ratio 2.
